one_line_takeaway: CRAFT aligns a reasoning model's hidden-state trajectory, not just its output, using contrastive latent structuring plus a GRPO consistency reward, cutting both unsafe reasoning traces (82.1%) and unsafe final responses (89.6%) over base models while slightly improving math/code accuracy.
key_finding: On the two backbones averaged, CRAFT improves reasoning-trace safety by 82.1% and final-response safety by 89.6% over the base models, while also improving average math/code Pass@1 by 8.0% (i.e., no capability tax; it slightly helps). It achieves the best average defense among 7 methods: CRAFT average defense score 0.062 (DeepSeek) and 0.099 (Qwen3) in Table 1, beating the strongest baseline IPO (0.084 / 0.130). Ablations show the Latent-Textual Consistency reward (R_cons) is the single most important reward — removing it raises the average jailbreak score by 27.2%; removing R_ls +25.4%, R_txt +18.0%; removing the whole LCLR stage is worst at +32.4%. Under stronger automated attacks (GPTFuzzer, AutoDAN), CRAFT still improves reasoning safety by 72.1% and final-response safety by 85.9%.
core_contribution: The paper proposes CRAFT (Contrastive Reasoning Alignment), a post-training red-teaming alignment framework for large reasoning models (LRMs) that targets 'superficial safety alignment' (SSA) — the failure mode where a model emits a safe final refusal while its internal chain-of-thought still traverses unsafe reasoning states. Unlike prior output-level or SFT-based defenses, CRAFT operates over the hidden-state (latent) space of reasoning traces. It has two stages: (1) LCLR (Latent Contrastive Learning for Reasoning), which uses margin-based prototype/triplet losses plus SimCLR-style instance invariance and a safety-calibration loss to geometrically separate Safe / Rethink / Unsafe reasoning traces on a normalized latent hypersphere; and (2) R2L (Reinforcement over Reasoning Latents), a GRPO-based RL stage with three rewards — a Latent Semantic Reward (cosine distance of hidden state to safe/unsafe/rethink prototypes), a Textual Safety Reward (derived from StrongReject score of the final answer), and a Latent-Textual Consistency Reward (1 - L1 distance between latent-predicted safety prob and textual safety prob). The paper also gives a theoretical claim (Prop. 5.1) that adding the consistency reward with positive weight excludes SSA policies as local optima of GRPO.
threat_model: Adversary submits jailbreak / directly-malicious prompts (including templated jailbreaks and automated attacks like PAP, PAIR, GPTFuzzer, AutoDAN) to a large reasoning model. The specific risk CRAFT defends against is not just an unsafe final answer but harmful information leaking through the model's visible internal reasoning trace even when the final response is a safe refusal (superficial safety alignment). Defender has white-box access: they can extract hidden states, attach projection/safety heads, and run RL post-training on the model.
limitations: The authors' own Impact Statement notes the latent-alignment techniques could be misused to strengthen adversarial attacks and may reduce generalizability or exacerbate bias. Section 6.1 concedes CRAFT is not top-ranked in every individual setting — on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B several SFT baselines (STAR, RealSafe, IPO) beat it on specific reasoning or response columns — which the authors attribute to a limited training budget. Reviewer-observed limitations: evaluation covers only two backbones (4B/8B, plus a 0.6B probe) and one training source (R2D-R1, 2,500 prompts), so scale and data-diversity generalization are untested; safety labels and reasoning-safety scoring depend on other models (Llama-Guard, GPT-4o judge, model self-evaluation), so the metric shares failure modes with the judges; the theoretical SSA-exclusion result (Prop. 5.1) rests on strong assumptions (Lipschitz heads, GRPO local optimality, local controllability, fixed textual evaluator) and only bounds expected latent-textual gap by C*epsilon rather than guaranteeing zero unsafe reasoning; and defense requires white-box RL post-training, so it does not protect deployed closed models.

datasets_used: JailbreakBench (100 directly-malicious prompts) — jailbreak defense eval
StrongReject (averaged over None/PAP/PAIR attack settings) — jailbreak defense eval and textual safety reward source
R2D-R1 (2,500 harmful prompts + benign prompts) — training data with paired reasoning-response safety annotations
GPTFuzzer and AutoDAN — stronger automated jailbreak robustness eval
quantitative_results: Average safety improvement over base models: 82.1% reasoning-trace safety, 89.6% final-response safety (across DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B and Qwen3-4B-Thinking)
Average math/code Pass@1 improvement over base: 8.0%
Table 1 average defense score (lower better): CRAFT 0.062 (DeepSeek), 0.099 (Qwen3); best baseline IPO 0.084 / 0.130; Base 0.567 / 0.524
CRAFT vs strongest baselines: +18.1% reasoning-trace safety, +38.3% final-response safety
Ablation (avg jailbreak score increase vs full CRAFT): remove LCLR +32.4%, remove R_cons +27.2%, remove R_ls +25.4%, remove R_txt +18.0%
Under GPTFuzzer/AutoDAN: +72.1% reasoning-trace safety, +85.9% final-response safety

Latent geometry: RETHINK projection coefficient alpha in (0,1) across 4 models (0.53 Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, 0.63 Qwen3-0.6B, 0.79 DeepSeek-Llama, 0.83 Qwen3-4B-Thinking), confirming transitional position on the SAFE->UNSAFE axis
Table 2 average capability (Pass@1, higher better): CRAFT 0.481 (DeepSeek) / 0.602 (Qwen3) vs Base 0.437 / 0.569
baselines_compared: SafeChain (SFT with structured safety refusal chains)
RealSafe (SFT with distilled DeepSeek-R1 safety reasoning traces)
STAR (fine-tune on self-generated policy-aligned reasoning traces)
SafeKey (extends STAR with extra reasoning-safety supervision)
IPO (intervention-based preference optimization, strongest overall baseline)
